one_line_takeaway: A black-box, multi-agent RL jailbreak that replaces proxy-answer rewards with a 'vocabulary-richness' (IQ) signal, achieving near-saturated attack success on 13 LLMs, including strongly-aligned commercial models, at roughly a tenth of GPTFuzzer's query cost.
key_finding: Across 13 commercial/open-source models under the GPT-4o-mini evaluator (Table 4), PathSeeker reaches an average Top1-ASR of 98.9% and Top5-ASR of 99.7% (average excludes Claude-3.5-sonnet), winning best Top1-ASR on 13/13 models and best Top5-ASR on 11/13, versus GPTFuzzer's 94.5% average Top1-ASR and ReNeLLM's 52.3%. It is markedly stronger on the hardest strongly-aligned commercial target Claude-3.5-sonnet (82% Top1 / 95% Top5) where all five baselines nearly fail (e.g., GPTFuzzer 0% Top1, Cipher 2%). It does this at ~9% of GPTFuzzer's query cost (4,500 total requests vs 50,000). Ablations show multi-agent MADDPG beats single-agent DQN and no-RL (on Llama-2-7b Top1-ASR 98% vs 78% vs 74%), and that combining IQ and judge rewards plus dual-pool mutation beats each component alone.
core_contribution: PathSeeker is a black-box LLM jailbreak method that frames attacking an aligned model as a rat escaping a per-model 'security maze'. Its two concrete novelties are: (1) a multi-agent reinforcement-learning controller in which two independent small agents (a 'question agent' and a 'template agent'), each with 5 discrete mutation actions and jointly optimized with MADDPG, decide how to mutate a harmful question and a jailbreak template each iteration; and (2) a reward that needs NO harmful reference answer. The reward combines an Information Quantization (IQ) signal, which counts nouns/verbs/adjectives/adverbs in response subsentences related to the input (rewarding growing 'vocabulary richness' as a proxy for the model relaxing its safety constraints), with a judgement-model confidence score. The paper claims to be the first to use multi-agent RL to guide small mutation models against a target LLM without relying on proxy-model reference answers. Distinguishing this from cited RL jailbreak work (RLbreaker, RL-JACK, RLTA) that computes rewards via BLEU/cosine similarity to proxy-generated harmful answers is the paper's central positioning, and must not be attributed to those prior works.
threat_model: Black-box adversary with query-only access to a target LLM. The attacker observes only input prompts and final output text (no weights, hidden states, or output logits), so the method applies uniformly to closed- and open-source models. The attacker maintains a pool of harmful questions and seed jailbreak templates and iteratively mutates them via an auxiliary mutation LLM steered by trained RL agents, with the goal of eliciting harmful/unsafe content from safety-aligned models. Attacker resources include an auxiliary mutation LLM (GPT-4o-mini), a judgement LLM, a small embedding model, and thousands of target queries.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) high wall-clock time cost per attack because each iteration runs RL strategy selection plus LLM-based mutation, so total runtime exceeds simpler baselines despite fewer queries; effectiveness also depends heavily on the manually crafted seed templates (inherited from GPTFuzzer). (2) Substantial nondeterminism because mutators, judge, IQ extractor, and RL agents all involve stochastic components. (3) Attack strategies may not fully transfer across dissimilar model types, especially models specifically defended against its mutation styles. Reviewer-noted caveats grounded in the text: the IQ reward is a heuristic proxy (POS-word richness), and both the judge and the attack evaluator are the same GPT-4o-mini family, a shared-model bias only partially mitigated by Llama Guard3 cross-validation, under which scores drop (avg Top1-ASR 92.2%, and Claude Top1 falls to 45%); transfer to the largest target (Llama-3.1-405b) is the weakest (template transfer 64% Top1).

datasets_used: 100 harmful questions from GPTFuzzer (manually crafted or crowdsourced)

13 target LLMs: GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4o-mini, Claude-3.5-sonnet, Deepseek-chat, Deepseek-coder, Gemini-1.5-flash, GLM-4-air, Qwen2-7b-instruct, Gemma2-8b-instruct, Mistral-nemo, Llama-2-7b-chat, Llama-2-13b-chat, Llama-3.1-8b (plus Llama-3-70b, Llama-3.1-70b, Llama-3.1-405b used in RQ5 transfer)
quantitative_results: PathSeeker average Top1-ASR 98.9% / Top5-ASR 99.7% across 13 models under GPT-4o-mini evaluator (Table 4, average excludes Claude-3.5-sonnet)
Best Top1-ASR on 13/13 models and best Top5-ASR on 11/13 models (GPT-4o-mini evaluator)
Claude-3.5-sonnet: PathSeeker 82% Top1 / 95% Top5 vs GPTFuzzer 0% Top1 / 0% Top5, Cipher 2% Top1, CodeChameleon-Text 32% Top1
Under Llama Guard3-8b cross-evaluation: PathSeeker average Top1-ASR 92.2% / Top5-ASR 97.9%; best Top1 on 10/13, best Top5 on 7/13; Claude 45% Top1 / 78% Top5
Baseline averages (GPT-4o-mini evaluator): GPTFuzzer 94.5% Top1 / 100% Top5; ReNeLLM 52.3% Top1 / 68.7% TopN; Jailbroken 52.9% Top1; CodeChameleon-Text 58.2% Top1 / Code 66.7% Top1
Efficiency: 4,500 total requests (3,000 iteration + 1,500 ASR) = 9% of GPTFuzzer's 50,000 requests (500 iterations)
RQ2 ablation Top1-ASR (Table 2): Llama-2-7b no-RL 74% / DQN 78% / MADDPG 98%; GPT-3.5-turbo 97% / 98% / 100%
RQ3 ablation Top1-ASR (Table 3): full PathSeeker 98% (Llama-2-7b) and 100% (GLM-4-air) vs r_IQ-only 70%/89%, r_J-only 74%/88%, template-only 77%/93%

RQ1 mutation-model choice on Llama-2-7b (Table 1): Qwen2-72b 36.67% Top1 (48.6s), Deepseek-chat 30.51% (25.07s), GPT-4o-mini 28.33% (16.01s, fastest, selected), Llama-3.1-70b 22.03%, Vicuna-7b 11.87% (6.5s)

RQ5 transfer (Table 6): template transfer Top1-ASR Llama-3.1-8b 97%, Llama-3.1-70b 91%, Llama-3.1-405b 64%; agent-weight transfer improves over no-transfer (e.g., Llama-3.1-405b 55% vs 44% Transfer-Top1)
Manual verification of 50 randomly sampled evaluation results reported at 100% accuracy
Reward hyperparameters: alpha=0.5, UCB delta=0.95; embedding model all-MiniLM-L6-v2 = 23M params; 3,000 training rounds
